skill.name: routeros-container
skill.description: RouterOS /container subsystem for running OCI containers on MikroTik devices. Use when: enabling containers on RouterOS, setting up VETH/bridge networking for containers, managing container lifecycle via CLI or REST API, building OCI images for RouterOS, configuring container environment variables, troubleshooting container issues, or when the user mentions RouterOS container, /container, VETH, device-mode container, or MikroTik Docker.
skill.summary: Run OCI containers on MikroTik devices with RouterOS 7.x container subsystem
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Documentation-only skill containing RouterOS container subsystem reference material. Static analysis flagged 171 patterns (external commands, network access, filesystem), but evaluation confirms all are false positives: CLI command paths, network examples, Base64 auth encoding, and relative documentation links are all legitimate documentation patterns. No executable code, no malicious intent, no attack patterns detected.

content.user_title: Configure RouterOS Containers on MikroTik Devices
content.value_statement: Managing containerized applications on MikroTik routers requires understanding RouterOS 7.x container subsystem, VETH networking, and OCI image requirements. This skill provides complete reference documentation for CLI commands, REST API patterns, and network configuration.
actual_capabilities: Enable device-mode and install container package on RouterOS 7.x
Configure VETH interfaces and bridge networking for containers
Create and manage container lifecycle via RouterOS CLI
Pull OCI images from registries or import local tar files
Set container environment variables and volume mounts
Interact with container subsystem via REST API with authentication
limitations: Requires RouterOS 7.x with container extra package installed
ARM, ARM64, or x86 architecture required (MIPS not supported)
Device-mode requires physical access for initial activation

use_cases.title: Network Admin Deploying Pi-hole DNS Sinkhole
use_cases.description: Network administrators can deploy Pi-hole inside a RouterOS container to block ads and tracking at the network level, using VETH bridge networking for container internet access.

use_cases.title: DevOps Engineer Setting Up Container Infrastructure
use_cases.description: DevOps engineers can automate container deployment on multiple MikroTik devices using REST API examples for integration with existing CI/CD pipelines.

use_cases.title: IT Professional Troubleshooting Container Issues
use_cases.description: IT professionals can diagnose container problems using log commands and API status checks to verify running state and troubleshoot networking issues.

prompt_templates.title: Enable Container Package
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I enable the container subsystem on RouterOS 7.x? Show me the commands to install the container package and enable device-mode.
prompt_templates.scenario: Initial container setup on a new MikroTik device

prompt_templates.title: Create Container with Networking
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a VETH interface and bridge for a new container. The container should use 172.17.0.2/24 with gateway 172.17.0.1.
prompt_templates.scenario: Setting up networking for a container

prompt_templates.title: Pull Image and Start Container
prompt_templates.prompt: Pull the pihole/pihole:latest image from Docker Hub and start it with environment variables TZ=America/New_York and WEBPASSWORD=secret.
prompt_templates.scenario: Deploying a container image from registry

prompt_templates.title: Automate Container Management via REST API
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a TypeScript function that lists all containers, checks their running status, and stops any containers that are running for more than 24 hours.

output_examples.input: How do I enable containers on RouterOS?
output_examples.output: To enable containers on RouterOS 7.x: 1) Upload the container package via SCP to the router. 2) Run /system/package/apply-changes to install it. 3) Enable device-mode with /system/device-mode/update mode=advanced container=yes. 4) Physically confirm by pressing reset button or power cycling. 5) Verify with /system/package/print where name=container.

output_examples.input: Set up networking for my container with IP 172.17.0.2
output_examples.output: Create VETH interface: /interface/veth/add name=veth-myapp address=172.17.0.2/24 gateway=172.17.0.1. Create bridge: /interface/bridge/add name=containers. Add VETH to bridge: /interface/bridge/port/add bridge=containers interface=veth-myapp. Assign gateway IP: /ip/address/add address=172.17.0.1/24 interface=containers. Add NAT: /ip/firewall/nat/add chain=srcnat action=masquerade src-address=172.17.0.0/24.

Check container status: /container/print. Verify logging is enabled and check logs: /log/print where topics~container. Ensure container tar is in correct root-dir path. Verify VETH interface exists: /interface/veth/print. Check if device-mode is still enabled after reboot.

best_practices: Always place container volumes on external storage (disk1/) rather than internal flash to avoid wearing out the router storage
Use inline environment variables (7.21+) for self-contained container definitions that are easier to manage than separate named lists
Enable logging=yes during initial setup to troubleshoot issues, then disable for production to save storage space
anti_patterns: Do not use /system/reboot for package activation on RouterOS 7.18+; use /system/package/apply-changes instead or uploaded packages will be discarded
Do not delete a container while it is still running; stop it first by polling .running until it shows 'false' or deletion will fail with HTTP 400
Do not pull multi-layer images or gzipped layers; RouterOS only supports single-layer uncompressed Docker v1 tar format for local imports

faq.question: What RouterOS version is required for containers?
faq.answer: RouterOS 7.x with the container extra package installed is required. The device must be ARM, ARM64, or x86 architecture. MIPS devices do not support containers.

faq.question: How is RouterOS container different from Docker?
faq.answer: RouterOS containers are NOT Docker. It uses a proprietary OCI-compatible implementation with specific requirements: single-layer uncompressed tar files, Docker v1 manifest format, and VETH networking instead of bridge networking by default.

faq.question: How do I authenticate to the RouterOS REST API?
faq.answer: Use HTTP Basic Authentication with Base64 encoding. Encode credentials as btoa('username:password') and include in the Authorization header. Example: Authorization: Basic YWRtaW46cGFzc3dvcmQ=.

faq.question: Why is my container showing .running as 'true' but not working?
faq.answer: The .running field returns string values 'true'/'false', not JavaScript booleans. Use === 'true' for comparison. Also ensure the container has a valid VETH interface and proper network configuration.

faq.question: Can I run multiple containers on the same MikroTik router?
faq.answer: Yes, RouterOS supports multiple containers. Each container needs its own VETH interface. Add multiple VETH interfaces to the same bridge to allow inter-container communication.

faq.question: Where should I store container images and data?
faq.answer: Always store container data on external storage (disk1/) rather than internal flash storage. Containers can require significant IOPS and storage space. Use /system/package/print to verify available storage before pulling images.
